info.title: getting started
info.text: In this demo we'll use MediaPipe Hands, an open source machine learning model by Google, to track hand pose in real time from your webcam. We'll use the positions of your fingertips to draw on screen and map the distance between your fingers to the page's background color.

info.title: libraries
info.text: We're importing four libraries. The first is the familiar nn library. The other three download the functions necessary for running MediaPipe models in your browser: the core TensorFlow.js library, the hand-tracking pipeline and the hand-pose wrapper.

info.title: global vars
info.text: We declare three variables at the top level: model will hold the loaded AI model, video will be the webcam feed element, and canvas will be the drawing surface we overlay on top of it. Making them global keeps them accessible across both the setup and update functions.

info.title: the start button
info.text: Rather than running immediately on page load, we create a button with the nn library and wire it to call setup on click. This matters because requesting webcam access, which happens inside setup, requires a user gesture in all modern browsers. The button provides that gesture.

info.title: webcam video
info.text: The setup function is async because it needs to await a few things. The first is creating a video element and feeding it a webcam stream using nn.askFor(), which prompts the browser's camera permission dialog and returns a MediaStream.

info.title: mirror the feed
info.text: We set its position to (20, 20) and opacity to 0.5 so it sits neatly behind the canvas drawings. We also call .scale(-1, 1) on the video element to flip it horizontally on the x-axis (while leaving the vertical y-axis alone). This makes the webcam feed behave like a mirror. Without it the feed could feel reversed and disorienting.

info.title: canvas overlay
info.text: We then create a canvas element positioned at the same coordinates as the video (20, 20) and add it on top. This is where we'll draw the hand markers. By layering the canvas over a semi-transparent video feed we get a simple augmented-reality effect.

info.title: drawing styles
info.text: Before we start drawing we configure the canvas's drawing styles: lineWidth sets the stroke thickness, strokeColor sets the line color to white, and fillColor is set to 'transparent' so our circles are outlines rather than filled shapes.

info.title: loading the model
info.text: Next we load the hand-tracking model, first by specifying the type, then the config object containing details like the "solutionPath" (URL to the actual model weights), whether we want the "full" or "lite" model (less accurate) and how many hands we want to track. Lastly, the .createDetector() method downloads the model locally and stores the detector in our model variable.

info.title: start the loop
info.text: After everything is initialized, this.remove() deletes the start button from the page since we no longer need it (this keyword refers to the button here because that's what called the setup()), and then update() is called once to kick off the render loop.

info.title: the update loop
info.text: The update function is also async because it awaits the model's inference (aka prediction) each frame. Calling requestAnimationFrame, which tells the browser to call update recursively, roughly 60 times per second. This creates a continuous loop that keeps the hand tracking live.

info.title: sync canvas size
info.text: The webcam video may take a moment to report its actual pixel dimensions after it starts. This check ensures the canvas stays in sync with the video's size. If they differ, canvas.resize() corrects it, otherwise the drawings would be misaligned with the video feed.

info.title: estimateHands
info.text: We run canvas.clear() each frame to erase it, then run model.estimateHands(). We pass it the current video frame as well as an opts object which flips the image to match the mirrored video. The model analyzes the current frame and returns an array of detected hands, We grab the first detected hand with hands[0], it contains 21 keypoints representing joints and fingertips.

info.title: keypoints
info.text: If a hand is detected, we extract two keypoints from its keypoints array: index 8 is the index fingertip and index 4 is the thumb tip. Each keypoint has an x and y property giving its pixel coordinates on the video frame. The full 21 points hand skeleton covers every finger joint, so you could track far more than just these two points.

info.title: drawing landmarks
info.text: We draw a circle at each fingertip with canvas.circle() and connect them with canvas.line(). These are nn canvas helper methods, they map to the underlying Canvas API but with a simpler interface.

info.title: distance to color
info.text: Finally, we use nn.dist() to calculate the pixel distance between the two fingertips and pass that value into nn.rgb(d, d, d). Because all three color channels are equal, this produces a shade of gray. When your fingers are close together the background is near black; spread them apart and it brightens toward white. It's a simple example of mapping body motion to a visual parameter.

info.title: lots more
info.text: This demo uses just two of the 21 available keypoints, you could also change maxHands: 1 to 2 to track both hands simultaneously. Hand tracking is just one of the open source TensorFlow.js models you can run locally in the browser, keeping data private on-device and reducing the need for energy-intensive datacenter processing.
